content.full_text:
DRAFT 2-9
REL:km

Patrick C. Coughlan
Westlake Village City Attorney
Richards, Watson, Dreyfuss & Gershon
Thirty-Eighth Floor
333 South Hope Street
Los Angeles, CA 90071

Re: Request for Written Advice, Our No. A-82-036

Dear Mr. Coughlan:

Thank you for your request for written advice regarding
Westlake Village City Councilmembers Bonnie Klove and Irwin
Shane. In addition to our mutual correspondence and numerous
telephone conversations, I have met with yourself and representatives of the other interested parties in this matter.
They are:

Mr. George Kimball, Attorney for Westpark Apartments
Mr. Terry Dowdall, Attorney for Oak Forest Mobile Estates
Mr. Raphael Chaikin, Co-Owner of Oak Forest Mobile Estates

I have also received a telephonic communication from
Mr. Henderson, a resident of Westlake Village. In addition,
I personally visited Westlake Village and toured Oak Forest
Mobile Estates, as well as visited Westpark Apartments. I
have also received numerous written communications from
Messrs. Kimball and Dowdall, as well as an appraisal of Oak
Forest submitted to Mr. Chaikin by Real Estate Analysts of
Newport, Inc. I have specifically invited counsel for all
parties to submit arguments on the applicability of various
provisions of the Political Reform Act and Commission interpretations thereof. All of the materials and data submitted
have been reviewed by me and considered in the analysis of
this matter. I have also consulted with, and received technical assistance as to legal interpretations of the California
Mobilehome Residency Law (infra) from Ronald S. Jayor, General
Counsel, State Department of Housing and Community Development,
the agency charged with the administration of that body of
law.

You seek written advice pursuant to Government Code
Section 83114(b) as to whether or not Councilmembers Klove
and Shane have any conflicts of interest relating to the
proposed adoption of a revised rent control ordinance, for
the City of Westlake Village.

FACTS

1. Bonnie Klove and Irwin Shane are City Councilmembers in
the City of Westlake Village, located in Los Angeles County.
Westlake Village was incorporated as a result of a vote at the
November 4, 1981, election.

- 1 -

2. The City is currently subject to a rent control ordinance
originally adopted by Los Angeles County (prior to incorporation
of Westlake Village) and subsequently re-adopted and extended by
the Westlake Village City Council (Councilmembers Klove and Shane
not participating). As extended, that ordinance will run through
June 30, 1985.

3. Ms. Klove and Mr. Shane each reside in Oak Forest Mobile
Estates (the "Park"), in Westlake Village. Oak Forest has a total
of 162 spaces, 160 of which are currently occupied. Ms. Klove
resides in Space 18, Mr. Shane in Space 160.

4. There is only one mobilehome park in Westlake Village -
Oak Forest Mobile Estates. There is only one apartment complex in
Westlake Village - Westpark Apartments. No other residential
rental units exist which are subject to coverage of the existing
rent control ordinance (single-family units are exempted), or the
proposed rent control ordinance (4-units or less are exempted).

5. The Westlake City Council is considering adopting an
alternative rent control ordinance which would differ in certain
respects from the existing ordinance. The material substantive
differences are as follows:

                          Existing              Proposed

a)  Duration         Until July 1, 1985,    Until July 1, 1987,
                     with optional re-      with mandatory annual
                     view at any time       review and required
                     Council desires.       findings; optional
                                           review at any time
                                           Council desires.

b)  Formula for      75% of CPI increase    75% of CPI increase
    Rent Adjust-     until 6/30/84, then    or 8% whichever is
    ments            100% of CPI increase.  lesser, until 6/30/87.

c)  Vacancy          Apts. - Decontrol upon Apts. - Permanent
    Decontrol        change in tenants -    decontrol upon vacancy.
                     then, after adjustment, Mobilehomes - no
                     recontrol. Mobilehomes decontrol upon change
                     - same.                in tenants, except
                                           if removal of mobile-
                                           home as well, then
                                           permanent decontrol.

6. The current status of Councilmember Klove's and Shane's
tenancy in the Park is as follows:


                          - 2 -

a) Klove's lease extension,(putative)1/ expires 4/30/82,
thereafter, by operation of law,2/ her tenancy devolves into
a month-to-month tenancy, or, again by operation of law,3/
she can elect to have a 12-month lease (or any shorter term
she elects) or to negotiate with the Park owner for a mutually
agreeable longer-term lease.

b) Shane's lease extension (putative)4/ expires 6/30/82,
thereafter the same provisions apply for Shane as above-stated
for Klove.

c) Under the current rent control ordinance, the rent,
upon expiration of these putative leases, will revert to the
level where it is now, consequently the existence or non-
existence of the putative leases will not affect future rents,
regardless of the proposed ordinance. The issue to be resolved
by the pending litigation5/ will only determine the amount of
back rent,6/ if any, owing to the Park from Klove and Shane
for the 33-month term of the putative lease extension. The
difference is approximately $50 per month.

_________________________

1/ There is pending litigation by the Park against Los Angeles
County and 17 space tenants in the Park over whether leases were
entered into which exempted those spaces from the application of
the County's original rent control ordinance. If exempted, rent
levels would increase in accordance with the terms of the lease
during the lease term, otherwise the levels would be controlled by
the ordinance. During the pendency of the litigation, the ordinance
controlled levels have applied. Regardless of the outcome of the
litigation, at the expiration of the term of the putative leases,
rents will be as if the leases had not existed, inasmuch as the
rents will revert to the controlled level.

2/ Mobilehome Residency Law, Civil Code Section 798, et seq.

3/ Id.

4/ See footnote 1.

5/ Because the (putative) lease extension periods will expire
before the end of the lease and because, upon expiration, the
rents will revert to the current levels as controlled by the current
ordinance, the only matter to be resolved by the litigation is
whether the 17 space tenants owe back rent for their respective
(putative) lease periods. The proposed ordinance will have no
effect on the litigation nor will the litigation have any effect
on the proposed ordinance.

6/ Id.

- 3 -

d) Klove's rent is currently $234 per month and Shane's
rent is currently $332 per month (ordinance controlled levels).

e) Under the current ordinance, if a mobilehome tenant
sells his/her mobilehome and a new tenant moves in, the rent
will be adjustable (upward presumably) if the previous tenant
is on a month-to-month tenancy or at the end of a fixed-term
lease. The rent would thereafter be controlled. However, if
the previous tenant is in the middle of the term of a fixed-
term lease, the new tenant would take possession subject to
that lease and would retain the existing level of rent. Upon
expiration of that lease term, the rent would be controlled
(not adjustable) under the current ordinance.

f) Under the proposed ordinance, if a mobilehome tenant
sells his/her mobilehome and a new tenant moves in, the rent
would remain controlled whether there is a lease or not and
it would become adjustable only if the mobilehome were removed
from the space and a new one brought in.

g) Park tenants, including Klove and Shane, are required
to install certain improvements and landscaping upon moving
onto a space in the Park. These improvements include a carport.
Approximate cost of these improvements averages $6,500. In
addition, many tenants have also installed customized decking
and other improvements. Legally, these improvements are not
fixtures and remain the personal property of the tenants and
may be removed by the tenants.

h) The mobilehomes in the Park are all of sufficient
age so as to be registered through the Department of Housing
and Community Development and display license plates; they
are subject to the annual mobilehome tax (formerly vehicle
use tax). None are affixed to permanent foundations.

i) The owners of the Park have prepared and submitted a
study comparing the "market rents"— and the current rents in
the Park.

j) The Park owners (and Mr. Kimball) submit that the
difference between the current rents and the "market rents," as
shown by their study, results in a "premium" in favor of the
Park tenants and that this "premium" can be capitalized
and sold by a Park tenant when selling his/her mobilehome
(when moving out but leaving the coach in place). Klove and
Shane do not accede to this contention. However, you do
stipulate on their behalf, that if such a premium does exist
and can result in a higher price for sale of their mobilehomes,
then that differential benefit would correlate with the relative
size of the "premium," all other things being equal.

7/ See definition of "market rents" in study. The owners
stipulate that even if there were no rent control in Westlake
Village, the Park would charge rents below those stated as "market"
rents in the study.

- 4 -

7.     The City of Westlake Village has a population of 11,500
residents contained in 2,370 housing units which consist of 162
mobile home park spaces, 234 apartments and the balance in single
family detached dwellings or condominiums.  Thus there are basically
400 rental units in Westlake Village subject to either of the two
ordinances.  Of the total residents of these two complexes is
approximately 200 in the apartment complex and 320 in the Park.

8.     Westpark Apartments is considering possible condominium
conversion and consequently 100 apartments are currently vacant
and being held off the market.

9.     The owners of the Park have given the required 12-month
notice to Park residents that they may wish to effectuate a change
of use in the property, possibly to condominiums.  This notice was
given "several years ago."

10.    Westlake Village is an upper middle income community.

11.    Councilmembers Klove and Shane have stated that they
could afford to remain residents of the Park if rents were in-
creased to the "market rents" in the study, but that for tax reasons
(the lack of any tax deduction on such high rents) and the lack of
any equity accumulation (or appreciation), they might consider
moving if rents did increase to that level.  However, they have no
present intention to sell their mobilehomes and move.

ANALYSIS

General Standard

     The Political Reform Act (hereinafter the "Act" or the
"PRA")8/  mandates that no public official may make or participate
in the making of any government decision in which he or she
knows or has reason to know that he or she has a financial
interest.2/

Financial Interest

     Section 87103 of the Act defines when an official has a
financial interest within the meaning of the Act.  It states
as follows:

     An official has a financial interest in a decision
within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably

________________
8/  Government Code Sections 81000-91014; hereinafter
all references are to the Government Code unless otherwise
specified.

9/  Section 87100.


                                                          - 5 -

design, dependent upon rent control decisions relative to
neighboring jurisdictions.

Lastly, Klove and Shane have indicated that they do not
plan to sell their mobilehomes and move, certainly not if
rent control (current or proposed) remains in effect. Therefore,
we must conclude that the direct effect test is not met and
there is no conflict.

Alternative 4b

Here the financial effect which is alleged by Messrs.
Kimball and Dowdall is an effect upon the market value of
Klove's and Shane's coaches. It is an appreciation (on
paper) in the value of their personalty. Although neither
Klove nor Shane plans to sell her or his coach, the effect
is just as real as an increase in the market value of real
property, which is on paper only until the property is actually
sold. Consequently, we hold that the financial effect on
Klove and Shane is direct and clearly exceeds $100 and,
therefore, a material financial effect exists.

Alternative 4c

Here the financial effect alleged by Messrs. Kimball
and Dowdall is disputed by the requesting parties. The
Commission is not a fact-finding body and our advice is
rendered upon the facts submitted by the requesting official(s).18/
Consequently, we find no material financial effect, based
upon the facts submitted to us by the requesting officials.

PUBLIC GENERALLY

Lastly, we must turn our attention to whether or not
the reasonably foreseeable effect of the proposed ordinance
upon Klove and Shane is distinguishable from its effect on
the public generally.19/ The Commission, by regulation has
refined the term public generally20/ include "a significant
segment of the public generally."20/ This term has, in

18/ In the letter to Thomas Toomey, supra, we did rely
on other facts; however, those facts were submitted by a
neutral, public official, whose official duties were to make
such evaluations.

19/ Section 87103 provides that an official does not
have a financial interest in a decision if the financial
effect upon the official's financial interest is not distinguishable
from the effect of the decision on the public generally.

20/ 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18703. "A material financial
effect of a governmental decision on an official's interests
... is distinguishable from its effect on the public generally
unless the decision will affect the official's interest in
substantially the same manner as it will affect all members
of the public or a significant segment of the public."

- 12 -

turn, been applied in several different circumstances in
Commission opinions and advice letters. Several of these
are helpful to our analysis in the instant case.

In particular, my advice letter to Paul H. Morgan, A-81-
507, dated December 2, 1981, contains a pertinent discussion.
There we advised as follows:

... Our regulation, 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18703
refines this term to include "a significant segment of
the public generally." While that phrase has never
been quantified by the Commission, it has been satisfied
by such segments as (1) owners of less than four units
of residential rental property, in Los Angeles;2/ (2) all
tenants, in Berkeley;3/ (3) homeowners, in Davis;4/
and shopowners who lease their shop space, in Davis.5/

A recurring analysis applied by the Commission to
any segment in question has been the extent to which
that segment is made up of diverse members, whose only
"common bond" is their membership in this grouping.6/
Turning to the segment in question here, mobile home
owners residing in mobile home parks in Westminster,
these households make up 10.9% or more of all households
in Westminister.7/ Mobile home owners are members of
virtually all occupations and are similarly diverse in
their other characteristics. Our advice would have
been that mobile home owners in Westminster's 17 parks
constituted a significant segment of the public generally.

_________________
2/ Ferraro, 4 FPPC Opinions 62, No. 78-009, November 7,
1978 (copy attached).

3/ Overstreet, supra.

4/ Owen, 2 FPPC Opinions 77, No. 76-005, June 2,
1976 (copy attached).

5/ Id.

6/ Ferraro, supra, at 66 and Overstreet, supra,
at 17-18.

7/ The vacancy rate for mobile home spaces is
smaller than for other housing units, consequently the
actual percentage of households is slightly higher than
the direct ratio of spaces to dwelling units. Mobile
home owners constitute 6.7% of the population of West-
minster; however, population was not the basis for the
Commission's analysis in Owen.


- 13 -

However, if the effect of Mr. Peterson's decision
was distinguishable from its effect on other mobile
home owners, then a conflict would exist. The ordinance
on its face affects all mobile home owners alike subject
to the future, contingent actions of groups of park
residents, "Park Committees," and arbitrators, as set
forth in the ordinance. As the Commission held in
Overstreet, supra, so long as the ordinance adoption
decision affected all the mobile home owners (as opposed
to only his park, or himself, etc.), then there would
have been no conflict of interest for Mr. Peterson.

8/
81.     Overstreet, supra at 18; and Owen, supra at

As stated in the Morgan letter, a significant segment
of the public generally has never been quantified by the
Commission. We do not seek to do so now. The concept is
broader than mere numbers. However, numbers have been sub-
mitted and argued extensively here. Consequently, we feel
it is incumbent upon us to at least address the question of
numbers in the course of our discussion.

The numbers here are as follows:

     (1)  Mobilehome households constitute 6.8% of the households
in Westlake Village.

     (2)  Apartments make up 9.9% of the households in Westlake
Village.

     (3)  Rental units total 16.7% of the households in
Westlake Village.

     (4)  Occupied renter households equal 13% of occupied
households in Westlake Village (assuming no vacancies in
nonrental households).

     (5)  Occupied mobilehome households equal 7% of occupied
households in Westlake Village (same assumption again).

As in previous advice, we have not chosen to use population
because rents are paid, units are leased (rented) and homes
are owned by household units rather than by individuals.
Our statistical "universe" has been the entire jurisdiction -
all 2,370 households units (except where we compared occupied
units in which instance we subtracted the 100 empty apartments
and the two vacant mobile home spaces).

     By comparison, in the Owen Opinion, the Commission
"concluded that homeowners in the redevelopment [core] area
of Davis constituted a significant segment of the public
because of their diverse nature and lack of group identity."

- 14 -

Ferraro, supra, 4 FPPC Opinions 62 at 67. A check with the
City of Davis Planning Department discloses that, at the time of the Owen Opinion, the number of households in the City (jurisdiction) was 11,729, the number of owner-occupied households was 5,682, with only 382 of those situated "within and in the immediate vicinity of the 'core area'." Owen Opinion, supra, 2 FPPC Opinions 77 at 81. Thus those households which were found to constitute a "significant segment" were 3.2% of all households in Davis and 6.7% of all owner-occupied households.

In the Ferraro Opinion, supra, the Commission declared that owners of three or fewer units of residential rental property constituted a significant segment of the public generally. There, the situation involved the City of Los Angeles. Through the Department of Housing and Community Development, a check with the City of Los Angeles Planning officials discloses that at the time of Ferraro there were 104,000 single-family rental structures; and 35,077 duplex structures containing a total of 52,607 rental units plus 17,554 owner-occupied units. There were 526,300 rental units in structures of more than two units. Some small number of these were tri-plexes. There were also 10,245 rented condominium units. Assuming that all of the structures and condominiums under three units were owned separately by City residents, then the percentage would be 12.5%. However, there is undoubtedly some multiple ownership causing one household to own four units or more, even though none are in structures containing more than three units. Also some owners will reside outside the jurisdiction. Thus, estimates are that the true percentage would have been below 10%, possibly as low as 5%.

Subsequent to Ferraro, advice letters were written to several smaller jurisdictions without delving into the numbers. The Commission, in Ferraro, indicated that it was appropriate to "draw a line" and that line was applied to jurisdictions of varying size.

On the other end of the numerical scale, in my letter to Stanley Remelmeyer, A-81-510, dated December 7, 1981, we advised that the investors in a cable TV franchise applicant did not constitute a significant segment of the public generally. The facts were that the City of Torrance had 50,500 households of which fewer than 500 would be investors in the franchise. Thus, the percentage there was less than 1%. In addition, these investors were engaged in a common financial enterprise and were much less heterogenous because of other requirements for becoming an investor.

Lastly, we turn to the Overstreet Opinion, supra, which, inter alia, held that residential tenants constituted the public generally. 6 FPPC Opinions 12 at 17-18. In so doing, the Commission recognized that, in point of fact, tenants will always be more numerous than landlords who own three or fewer units. For example, in Westlake Village there are

- 15 -

approximately 400 rental units(apartments plus mobile home
spaces). Assuming maximum numerosity of owners, there would
be 400 owners of one unit each; there would also be 400
tenant households. Assuming owners have three units each,
there would be 133 owners. Under Ferraro's line-drawing we
would hold these to constitute a significant segment of the
public generally (assuming no other common denominators).
Since tenant households are greater in number, then they,
too, are a significant segment of the public generally.

Gross numbers are not controlling on questions of the
"public generally." Just because there were 100,000 plus
owners of three units or less in Los Angeles does not mean
that there must be a like number of any similar segment of
the public in order for us to consider it significant. In
fact, in Westlake Village there couldn't be 100,000 plus in
any group. The "public generally" is tied to the jurisdiction
in question and is a relative concept which will vary numeric-
ally with the size of the jurisdiction. As Mr. Kimball
states in his March 31, 1982, letter:

     In a larger city, 3% of the population or 6.75% of
the housing stock might represent large absolute numbers.
Cf. Ferraro.... In Westlake Village the absolute numbers
are small, and the law must apply fairly and consistently
to all California.

     We believe that a relative, jurisdictional approach
achieves that goal. In Owen, the absolute numbers of retail
merchants in the Davis core area was small - 250. But Davis
is much smaller than Los Angeles. Westlake Village is smaller
yet.


     We conclude that tenants in Westlake Village are suffi-
ciently numerous and diverse so as to constitute a significant
segment of the public generally. We turn now to some related
issues raised by Messrs. Kimball and Dowdall.

Differential Effect

     In support of their contention that the effect of the
proposed ordinance on Klove and Shane will differ from other
Park tenants, Messrs. Dowdall and Chaikin have submitted the
rental appraisal study previously mentioned to show that the
"premium" which they contend will be generated by the elimination
of vacancy decontrol will differ from space to space. We
question somewhat the applicability of the study to the
question at hand since, by its own definition, the market
rate rents shown are not the rents which would actually be
charged new tenants if the current ordinance is not altered.
The study really would only be applicable if there were
currently no rent control in Westlake Village.

     However, even if we felt that the study was applicable,
we are not convinced that Klove and Shane are affected in
other than "substantially the same manner" as other Park

                              - 16 -

tenants. The facts in Ferraro are quite clear that each of
the three city councilmembers in question would be hit with
a different "negative premium"21/ as to his or her tenants by enactment
of the then-proposed ordinance. Councilman Bernardi's difference would be $200 per month; Councilman Gibson's, $250 per
month; and Councilman Ronka's, $125 to $175 per month. The
same types of individual differences would be true of all
other owners of three units or less. The effect of the
ordinance as to how much rents on their units would be frozen
below market value would depend on the timing and size of
the last rent increase preceding the effective date of the
toll-back and freeze. The Commission clearly did not perceive
these differences as singling out these three councilmembers.
The proposed ordinance on its face treats all equally and
that constitutes "substantially the same manner."

Subdivisions of the Segment of the Public Generally

     Messrs. Kimball and Dowdall have raised two issues
concerning whether Ms. Klove and Mr. Shane are, in reality,
merely members of a subgroup of the tenants of Westlake
Village. The thrust of their contentions is that either
this subgroup is so small so as to lose its status as a
"significant segment" or, in the alternative, that the effects
on Klove and Shane are distinguishable from the effects on a
significant segment of the public generally. Both of these
contentions are grounded in the fact that the proposed ordinance,
in contrast to the current ordinance, eliminates vacancy
decontrol as to mobilehome tenants and establishes total
vacancy decontrol as to apartment tenants.

     Messrs. Kimball and Dowdall argue that the effect of
elimination of vacancy decontrol upon Klove and Shane differs
from its effect upon their neighbors, thus making it both
distinguishable and narrowing any segment from which it is
not distinguishable. They also argue that it simply treats
apartment tenants differently than mobilehome tenants. With
this latter contention we agree. The ordinance on its face
clearly would distinguish between these two types of tenants
and therefore its effect on them may be distinguishable.
However, that leaves us with the remaining questions of
whether mobilehome tenants of the only Park in town constitute
a significant segment of the public generally and whether
Klove and Shane are affected "in substantially the same
manner" as their fellow Park tenants.

_________________________

     21/ The amount by which their rents would be "frozen"
below the market rate - resulting in a negative "premium" in
the same sense as that alleged for Klove and Shane as a
positive "premium."


                              - 17 -

Messrs. Kimball and Dowdall have stressed the fact that
Ms. Klove and Mr. Shane reside in the only mobilehome park
in Westlake Village and that, consequently, they would be
making a decision directly affecting their tenancies. It is
certainly the case that if there were ten parks in town, and
the proposed ordinance affected only their park, we would
conclude that, under fn.4 in Ferraro and under Overstreet,
this factor would remove them from the "public generally"
exception. However, here the facts are unique in that there
is but one park in town.

Alternative 1

The proposed ordinance, on its face, affects all mobilehome
parks the same. Clearly, if another park were developed
during the term of the ordinance, it would be covered. If
the current park were split in half, both halves would be
covered but there would then be two parks. Given the fact
that it is the only park in town, we do not consider the one-
park issue to be controlling. The question then is simply
whether or not the mobilehome tenants constitute a significant
segment of the public generally.

Alternative 1a

We feel that on balance they do. The group is otherwise
diverse and is sufficiently numerous in relation to the size
of the jurisdiction to meet the test.

Alternative 1b

We feel that, all things considered, they do not constitute
a significant segment of the public generally given the one-
park issue combined with their smallness in numbers. While
the one-park issue is not controlling, it is a factor which
combined with the other unique factors here gives rise to
our conclusion. This is a conclusion that does not have
general applicability beyond this very unique situation.

Alternative 2

The language in Ferraro, particularly fn.4 (4 FPPC 62
at 67) and in Overstreet makes it clear that an official may
not act where his particular rental situation is the only
one being affected. Consequently, Ms. Klove and Mr. Shane
may not participate so long as their park is singled out for
differential treatment in the ordinance. If the proposal
treated both apartment tenants and mobilehome tenants alike,
then the "public generally" exception would apply.

Alternative 2a

Because of our conclusion here we need not reach the
question of whether the mobilehome park tenants would otherwise
constitute a significant segment of the public generally.

- 18 -

Alternative 2b

     We feel, however, that, absent the one-park effect, the
mobilehome tenants in Westlake Village do (not) constitute a
significant segment of the public generally.

Alternative 3

     As to possible subgroups within the Park (those with
larger vs. smaller "premiums"), we have already addressed
this issue above in our discussion of Ferraro.  However,
Messrs. Kimball and Dowdall argue forcefully that Klove and
Shane will benefit more than their neighbors and, hence, a
subgroup is appropriate.  Our analysis does not bear this
out.  While it is true that Ms. Klove pays less in rent than
her neighbor in Space 19, it is not true that her "premium"
is excessively large.  A thorough review of the Appraisal
(supra) submitted shows the following for Oak Forest Mobile
Estates:

     1.  Average22/ Current (Controlled) Rent         = $310.92

     2.  Average23/ Current (Market) Rent             =   409.57

     3.  Average24/ Current "Premium"                 =    98.65

     4.  Average25/ Current "Premium"                 =   102.00

     Ms. Klove's "premium" is alleged to be $111.00 or slightly
on the high side, while Mr. Shane's is alleged to be $83.00
or slightly on the low side.  In either case, we find these
differences to not be material and see no basis for determining
that a subgrouping should be applied to the Park tenants.

                              CONCLUSION

             (Fill in your "bottom line")


_______________________

     22/ The "average" was calculated as the arithmetic
mean by totalling the actual rents and dividing by 159 (number
of rents listed).

     23/ The "average" was calculated as the arithmetic
mean by totalling the "market" rents in the appraisal and
dividing by 162 (number of spaces).

     24/ The "average" was calculated by subtracting actual
average rents from market average rents.

     25/ This "average" is the median for the "premiums,"
with 80 tenants having a lesser "premium" and 79 a greater
"premium."

                              - 19 -

sections.question: You seek written advice pursuant to Government Code Section 83114(b) as to whether or not Councilmembers Klove and Shane have any conflicts of interest relating to the proposed adoption of a revised rent control ordinance, for the City of Westlake Village.
sections.conclusion:
(Fill in your "bottom line")

_____________________

22/ The "average" was calculated as the arithmetic mean by totalling the actual rents and dividing by 159 (number of rents listed).

23/ The "average" was calculated as the arithmetic mean by totalling the "market" rents in the appraisal and dividing by 162 (number of spaces).

24/ The "average" was calculated by subtracting actual average rents from market average rents.

25/ This "average" is the median for the "premiums," with 80 tenants having a lesser "premium" and 79 a greater "premium."
sections.facts:
1. Bonnie Klove and Irwin Shane are City Councilmembers in the City of Westlake Village, located in Los Angeles County. Westlake Village was incorporated as a result of a vote at the November 4, 1981, election. 2. The City is currently subject to a rent control ordinance originally adopted by Los Angeles County (prior to incorporation of Westlake Village) and subsequently re-adopted and extended by the Westlake Village City Council (Councilmembers Klove and Shane not participating). As extended, that ordinance will run through June 30, 1985. 3. Ms. Klove and Mr. Shane each reside in Oak Forest Mobile Estates (the "Park"), in Westlake Village. Oak Forest has a total of 162 spaces, 160 of which are currently occupied. Ms. Klove resides in Space 18, Mr. Shane in Space 160. 4. There is only one mobilehome park in Westlake Village - Oak Forest Mobile Estates. There is only one apartment complex in Westlake Village - Westpark Apartments. No other residential rental units exist which are subject to coverage of the existing rent control ordinance (single-family units are exempted), or the proposed rent control ordinance (4-units or less are exempted). 5. The Westlake City Council is considering adopting an alternative rent control ordinance which would differ in certain respects from the existing ordinance. The material substantive differences are as follows:
Existing Proposed
a) Duration Until July 1, 1985, with optional review at any time Council desires. Until July 1, 1987, with mandatory annual review and required findings; optional review at any time Council desires.
b) Formula for Rent Adjustments 75% of CPI increase until 6/30/84, then 100% of CPI increase. 75% of CPI increase or 8% whichever is lesser, until 6/30/87.
c) Vacancy Decontrol Apts. - Decontrol upon change in tenants - then, after adjustment, recontrol. Mobilehomes - same. Apts. - Permanent decontrol upon vacancy. Mobilehomes - no decontrol upon change in tenants, except if removal of mobile-home as well, then permanent decontrol.
6. The current status of Councilmember Klove's and Shane's tenancy in the Park is as follows: a) Klove's lease extension (putative) expires 4/30/82, thereafter, by operation of law, her tenancy devolves into a month-to-month tenancy, or, again by operation of law, she can elect to have a 12-month lease (or any shorter term she elects) or to negotiate with the Park owner for a mutually agreeable longer-term lease. b) Shane's lease extension (putative) expires 6/30/82, thereafter the same provisions apply for Shane as above-stated for Klove. c) Under the current rent control ordinance, the rent, upon expiration of these putative leases, will revert to the level where it is now, consequently the existence or non-existence of the putative leases will not affect future rents, regardless of the proposed ordinance. The issue to be resolved by the pending litigation will only determine the amount of back rent, if any, owing to the Park from Klove and Shane for the 38-month term of the putative lease extension. The difference is approximately $50 per month. 1/ There is pending litigation by the Park against Los Angeles County and 17 space tenants in the Park over whether leases were entered into which exempted those spaces from the application of the County's original rent control ordinance. If exempted, rent levels would increase in accordance with the terms of the lease during the lease term, otherwise the levels would be controlled by the ordinance. During the pendency of the litigation, the ordinance controlled levels have applied. Regardless of the outcome of the litigation, at the expiration of the term of the putative leases, rents will be as if the leases had not existed, inasmuch as the rents will revert to the controlled level. 2/ Mobilehome Residency Law, Civil Code Section 798, et seq. 3/ Id. 4/ See footnote 1. 5/ Because the (putative) lease extension periods will expire before the end of the lease and because, upon expiration, the rents will revert to the current levels as controlled by the current ordinance, the only matter to be resolved by the litigation is whether the 17 space tenants owe back rent for their respective (putative) lease periods. The proposed ordinance will have no effect on the litigation nor will the litigation have any effect on the proposed ordinance. 6/ Id. d) Klove's rent is currently $234 per month and Shane's rent is currently $332 per month (ordinance controlled levels). e) Under the current ordinance, if a mobilehome tenant sells his/her mobilehome and a new tenant moves in, the rent will be adjustable (upward presumably) if the previous tenant is on a month-to-month tenancy or at the end of a fixed-term lease. The rent would thereafter be controlled. However, if the previous tenant is in the middle of the term of a fixed-term lease, the new tenant would take possession subject to that lease and would retain the existing level of rent. Upon expiration of that lease term, the rent would be controlled (not adjustable) under the current ordinance. f) Under the proposed ordinance, if a mobilehome tenant sells his/her mobilehome and a new tenant moves in, the rent would remain controlled whether there is a lease or not and it would become adjustable only if the mobilehome were removed from the space and a new one brought in. g) Park tenants, including Klove and Shane, are required to install certain improvements and landscaping upon moving onto a space in the Park. These improvements include a carport. Approximate cost of these improvements averages $6,500. In addition, many tenants have also installed customized decking and other improvements. Legally, these improvements are not fixtures and remain the personal property of the tenants and may be removed by the tenants. h) The mobilehomes in the Park are all of sufficient age so as to be registered through the Department of Housing and Community Development and display license plates; they are subject to the annual mobilehome tax (formerly vehicle use tax). None are affixed to permanent foundations. i) The owners of the Park have prepared and submitted a study comparing the "market rents"7/ and the current rents in the Park. j) The Park owners (and Mr. Kimball) submit that the difference between the current rents and the "market rents," as shown by their study, results in a "premium" in favor of the Park tenants and that this "premium" can be capitalized and sold by a Park tenant when selling his/her mobilehome (when moving out but leaving the coach in place). Klove and Shane do not accede to this contention. However, you do stipulate on their behalf, that if such a premium does exist and can result in a higher price for sale of their mobilehomes, then that differential benefit would correlate with the relative size of the "premium," all other things being equal. 7/ See definition of "market rents" in study. The owners stipulate that even if there were no rent control in Westlake Village, the Park would charge rents below those stated as "market" rate rents in the study. 7. The City of Westlake Village has a population of 11,500 residents contained in 2,370 housing units which consist of 162 mobilehome park spaces, 234 apartments and the balance in single-family detached dwellings or condominiums. Thus there are basically 400 rental units in Westlake Village subject to either of the two ordinances. The total residents of these two rental complexes is approximately 200 in the apartment complex and 320 in the Park. 8. Westpark Apartments is considering possible condominium conversion and consequently 100 apartments are currently vacant and being held off the market. 9. The owners of the Park have given the required 12-month notice to Park residents that they may wish to effectuate a change of use in the property, possibly to condominiums. This notice was given "several years ago." 10. Westlake Village is an upper middle income community. 11. Councilmembers Klove and Shane have stated that they could afford to remain residents of the Park if rents were increased to the "market rents" in the study, but that for tax reasons (the lack of any tax deduction on such high rents) and the lack of any equity accumulation (or appreciation), they might consider moving if rents did increase to that level. However, they have no present intention to sell their mobilehomes and move.
sections.analysis:
General Standard

The Political Reform Act (hereinafter the "Act" or the "PRA") mandates that no public official may make or participate in the making of any government decision in which he or she knows or has reason to know that he or she has a financial interest.8/

Financial Interest

Section 87103 of the Act defines when an official has a financial interest within the meaning of the Act. It states as follows:

An official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably

; hereinafter all references are to the Government Code unless otherwise specified.
9/ Section 87100.

design, dependent upon rent control decisions relative to neighboring jurisdictions.

Lastly, Klove and Shane have indicated that they do not plan to sell their mobilehomes and move, certainly not if rent control (current or proposed) remains in effect. Therefore, we must conclude that the direct effect test is not met and there is no conflict.

Alternative 4b

Here the financial effect which is alleged by Messrs. Kimball and Dowdall is an effect upon the market value of Klove's and Shane's coaches. It is an appreciation (on paper) in the value of their personality. Although neither Klove nor Shane plans to sell her or his coach, the effect is just as real as an increase in the market value of real property, which is on paper only until the property is actually sold. Consequently, we hold that the financial effect on Klove and Shane is direct and clearly exceeds $100 and, therefore, a material financial effect exists.

Alternative 4c

Here the financial effect alleged by Messrs. Kimball and Dowdall is disputed by the requesting parties. The Commission is not a fact-finding body and our advice is rendered upon the facts submitted by the requesting official(s).18/ Consequently, we find no material financial effect, based upon the facts submitted to us by the requesting officials.

PUBLIC GENERALLY

Lastly, we must turn our attention to whether or not the reasonably foreseeable effect of the proposed ordinance upon Klove and Shane is distinguishable from its effect on the public generally.19/ The Commission, by regulation has refined the term public generally to include "a significant segment of the public generally."20/ This term has, in

18/ In the letter to Thomas Toomey, supra, we did rely on other facts; however, those facts were submitted by a neutral, public official, whose official duties were to make such evaluations.

19/ Section 87103 provides that an official does not have a financial interest in a decision if the financial effect upon the official's financial interest is not distinguishable from the effect of the decision on the public generally.

20/ 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18703. "A material financial effect of a governmental decision on an official's interests ... is distinguishable from its effect on the public generally unless the decision will affect the official's interest in substantially the same manner as it will affect all members of the public or a significant segment of the public."

turn, been applied in several different circumstances in Commission opinions and advice letters. Several of these are helpful to our analysis in the instant case.

In particular, my advice letter to Paul H. Morgan, A-81-507, dated December 2, 1981, contains a pertinent discussion. There we advised as follows:

... Our regulation, 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18703 refines this term to include "a significant segment of the public generally." While that phrase has never been quantified by the Commission, it has been satisfied by such segments as (1) owners of less than four units of residential rental property, in Los Angeles—(2) all tenants, in Berkeley;—(3) homeowners, in Davis;4/ and shopowners who lease their shop space, in Davis.—

A recurring analysis applied by the Commission to any segment in question has been the extent to which that segment is made up of diverse members, whose only "common bond" is their membership in this grouping.—Turning to the segment in question here, mobile home owners residing in mobile home parks in Westminster, these households make up 10.9% or more of all the households in Westminster.— Mobile home owners are members of virtually all occupations and are similarly diverse in their other characteristics. Our advice would have been that mobile home owners in Westminster's 17 parks constituted a significant segment of the public generally.

__________________________
2/ Ferraro, 4 FPPC Opinions 62, No. 78-009, November 7, 1978 (copy attached).
3/ Overstreet, supra.
4/ Owen, 2 FPPC Opinions 77, No. 76-005, June 2, 1976 (copy attached).
5/ Id.
6/ Ferraro, supra, at 66 and Overstreet, supra, at 17-18.
7/ The vacancy rate for mobile home spaces is smaller than for other housing units, consequently the actual percentage of households is slightly higher than the direct ratio of spaces to dwelling units. Mobile home owners constitute 6.7% of the population of Westminster; however, population was not the basis for the Commission's analysis in Owen.

However, if the effect of Mr. Peterson's decision was distinguishable from its effect on other mobile home owners, then a conflict would exist. The ordinance on its face affects all mobile home owners alike, subject to the future, contingent actions of groups of park residents, "Park Committees," and arbitrators, as set forth in the ordinance. As the Commission held in Overstreet, supra, so long as the ordinance adoption decision affected all the mobile home owners (as opposed to only his park, or himself, etc.), then there would have been no conflict of interest for Mr. Peterson.

8/ Overstreet, supra at 18; and Owen, supra at 81.

As stated in the Morgan letter, a significant segment of the public generally has never been quantified by the Commission. We do not seek to do so now. The concept is broader than mere numbers. However, numbers have been submitted and argued extensively here. Consequently, we feel it is incumbent upon us to at least address the question of numbers in the course of our discussion.

The numbers here are as follows:

(1) Mobilehome households constitute 6.8% of the households in Westlake Village.

(2) Apartments make up 9.9% of the households in Westlake Village.

(3) Rental units total 16.7% of the households in Westlake Village.

(4) Occupied renter households equal 13% of occupied households in Westlake Village (assuming no vacancies in nonrental households).

(5) Occupied mobilehome households equal 7% of occupied households in Westlake Village (same assumption again).

As in previous advice, we have not chosen to use population because rents are paid, units are leased (rented) and homes are owned by household units rather than by individuals. Our statistical "universe" has been the entire jurisdiction - all 2,370 households units (except where we compared occupied units in which instance we subtracted the 100 empty apartments and the two vacant mobilehome spaces).

By comparison, in the Owen Opinion, the Commission "concluded that homeowners in the redevelopment [core] area of Davis constituted a significant segment of the public because of their diverse nature and lack of group identity."

Ferraro, supra, 4 FPPC Opinions 62 at 67. A check with the City of Davis Planning Department discloses that, at the time of the Owen Opinion, the number of households in the City (jurisdiction) was 11,729, the number of owner-occupied households was 5,682, with only 382 of those situated "within and in the immediate vicinity of the 'core area'." Owen Opinion, supra, 2 FPPC Opinions 77 at 81. Thus those households which were found to constitute a "significant segment" were 3.2% of all households in Davis and 6.7% of all owner-occupied households.

In the Ferraro Opinion, supra, the Commission declared that owners of three or fewer units of residential rental property constituted a significant segment of the public generally. There, the situation involved the City of Los Angeles. Through the Department of Housing and Community Development, a check with the City of Los Angeles Planning officials discloses that at the time of Ferraro there were 104,000 single-family rental structures; and 35,077 duplex structures containing a total of 52,607 rental units plus 17,554 owner-occupied units. There were 526,300 rental units in structures of more than two units. Some small number of these were triplexes. There were also 10,245 rented condominium units. Assuming that all of the structures and condominiums under three units were owned separately by City residents, then the percentage would be 12.5%. However, there is undoubtedly some multiple ownership causing one household to own four units or more, even though none are in structures containing more than three units. Also some owners will reside outside the jurisdiction. Thus, estimates are that the true percentage would have been below 10%, possibly as low as 5%.

Subsequent to Ferraro, advice letters were written to several smaller jurisdictions without delving into the numbers. The Commission, in Ferraro, indicated that it was appropriate to "draw a line" and that line was applied to jurisdictions of varying size.

On the other end of the numerical scale, in my letter to Stanley Remelmeyer, A-81-510, dated December 7, 1981, we advised that the investors in a cable TV franchise applicant did not constitute a significant segment of the public generally. The facts were that the City of Torrance had 50,500 households of which fewer than 500 would be investors in the franchise. Thus, the percentage there was less than 1%. In addition, these investors were engaged in a common financial enterprise and were much less heterogenous because of other requirements for becoming an investor.

Lastly, we turn to the Overstreet Opinion, supra, which, inter alia, held that residential tenants constituted the public generally. 6 FPPC Opinions 12 at 17-18. In so doing, the Commission recognized that, in point of fact, tenants will always be more numerous than landlords who own three or fewer units. For example, in Westlake Village there are

approximately 400 rental units (apartments plus mobile home spaces). Assuming maximum numerosity of owners, there would be 400 owners of one unit each; there would also be 400 tenant households. Assuming owners have three units each, there would be 133 owners. Under Ferraro's line-drawing we would hold these to constitute a significant segment of the public generally (assuming no other common denominators). Since tenant households are greater in number, then they, too, are a significant segment of the public generally.

Gross numbers are not controlling on questions of the "public generally." Just because there were 100,000 plus owners of three units or less in Los Angeles does not mean that there must be a like number of any similar segment of the public in order for us to consider it significant. In fact, in Westlake Village there couldn't be 100,000 plus in any group. The "public generally" is tied to the jurisdiction in question and is a relative concept which will vary numerically with the size of the jurisdiction. As Mr. Kimball states in his March 31, 1982, letter:

In a larger city, 3% of the population or 6.75% of the housing stock might represent large absolute numbers. Cf. Ferraro.... In Westlake Village the absolute numbers are small, and the law must apply fairly and consistently to all California.

We believe that a relative, jurisdictional approach achieves that goal. In Owen, the absolute numbers of retail merchants in the Davis core area was small - 250. But Davis is much smaller than Los Angeles. Westlake Village is smaller yet.

We conclude that tenants in Westlake Village are sufficiently numerous and diverse so as to constitute a significant segment of the public generally. We turn now to some related issues raised by Messrs. Kimball and Dowdall.

Differential Effect

In support of their contention that the effect of the proposed ordinance on Klove and Shane will differ from other Park tenants, Messrs. Dowdall and Chaikin have submitted the rental appraisal study previously mentioned to show that the "premium" which they contend will be generated by the elimination of vacancy decontrol will differ from space to space. We question somewhat the applicability of the study to the question at hand since, by its own definition, the market rate rents shown are not the rents which would actually be charged new tenants if the current ordinance is not altered. The study really would only be applicable if there were currently no rent control in Westlake Village.

However, even if we felt that the study was applicable, we are not convinced that Klove and Shane are affected in other than "substantially the same manner" as other Park

tenants. The facts in Ferraro are quite clear that each of the three city councilmembers in question would be hit with a different "negative premium"21/ as to his rents by enactment of the then-proposed ordinance. Councilman Bernardi's difference would be $200 per month; Councilman Gibson's, $250 per month; and Councilman Ronka's, $125 to $175 per month. The same types of individual differences would be true of all other owners of three units or less. The effect of the ordinance as to how much rents on their units would be frozen below market value would depend on the timing and size of the last rent increase preceding the effective date of the roll-back and freeze. The Commission clearly did not perceive these differences as singling out these three councilmembers. The proposed ordinance on its face treats all equally and that constitutes "substantially the same manner."

Subdivisions of the Segment of the Public Generally

Messrs. Kimball and Dowdall have raised two issues concerning whether Ms. Klove and Mr. Shane are, in reality, merely members of a subgroup of the tenants of Westlake Village. The thrust of their contentions is that either this subgroup is so small so as to lose its status as a "significant segment" or, in the alternative, that the effects on Klove and Shane are distinguishable from the effects on a significant segment of the public generally. Both of these contentions are grounded in the fact that the proposed ordinance, in contrast to the current ordinance, eliminates vacancy decontrol as to mobilehome tenants and establishes total vacancy decontrol as to apartment tenants.

Messrs. Kimball and Dowdall argue that the effect of elimination of vacancy decontrol upon Klove and Shane differs from its effect upon their neighbors, thus making it both distinguishable and narrowing any segment from which it is not distinguishable. They also argue that it simply treats apartment tenants differently than mobilehome tenants. With this latter contention we agree. The ordinance on its face clearly would distinguish between these two types of tenants and therefore its effect on them may be distinguishable. However, that leaves us with the remaining questions of whether mobilehome tenants of the only Park in town constitute a significant segment of the public generally and whether Klove and Shane are affected "in substantially the same manner" as their fellow Park tenants.

21/ The amount by which their rents would be "frozen" below the market rate - resulting in a negative "premium" in the same sense as that alleged for Klove and Shane as a positive "premium."

Messrs. Kimball and Dowdall have stressed the fact that Ms. Klove and Mr. Shane reside in the only mobilehome park in Westlake Village and that, consequently, they would be making a decision directly affecting their tenancies. It is certainly the case that if there were ten parks in town, and the proposed ordinance affected only their park, we would conclude that, under fn.4 in Ferraro and under Overstreet, this factor would remove them from the "public generally" exception. However, here the facts are unique in that there is but one park in town.

Alternative 1

The proposed ordinance, on its face, affects all mobilehome parks the same. Clearly, if another park were developed during the term of the ordinance, it would be covered. If the current park were split in half, both halves would be covered but there would then be two parks. Given the fact that it is the only park in town, we do not consider the one-park issue to be controlling. The question then is simply whether or not the mobilehome tenants constitute a significant segment of the public generally.

Alternative 1a

We feel that on balance they do. The group is otherwise diverse and is sufficiently numerous in relation to the size of the jurisdiction to meet the test.

Alternative 1b

We feel that, all things considered, they do not constitute a significant segment of the public generally given the one-park issue combined with their smallness in numbers. While the one-park issue is not controlling, it is a factor which combined with the other unique factors here gives rise to our conclusion. This is a conclusion that does not have general applicability beyond this very unique situation.

Alternative 2

The language in Ferraro, particularly fn.4 (4 FPPC 62 at 67) and in Overstreet makes it clear that an official may not act where his particular rental situation is the only one being affected. Consequently, Ms. Klove and Mr. Shane may not participate so long as their Park is singled out for differential treatment in the ordinance. If the proposal treated both apartment tenants and mobilehome tenants alike, then the "public generally" exception would apply.

Alternative 2a

Because of our conclusion here we need not reach the question of whether the mobilehome park tenants would otherwise constitute a significant segment of the public generally.

Alternative 2b

We feel, however, that, absent the one-park effect, the mobilehome tenants in Westlake Village do (not) constitute a significant segment of the public generally.

Alternative 3

As to possible subgroups within the Park (those with larger vs. smaller "premiums"), we have already addressed this issue above in our discussion of Ferraro. However, Messrs. Kimball and Dowdall argue forcefully that Klove and Shane will benefit more than their neighbors and, hence, a subgroup is appropriate. Our analysis does not bear this out. While it is true that Ms. Klove pays less in rent than her neighbor in Space 19, it is not true that her "premium" is excessively large. A thorough review of the Appraisal (supra) submitted shows the following for Oak Forest Mobile Estates:

1. Average22/ Current (Controlled) Rent = $310.92
2. Average23/ Current (Market) Rent = 409.57
3. Average24/ Current "Premium" = 98.65
4. Average25/ Current "Premium" = 102.00

Ms. Klove's "premium" is alleged to be $111.00 or slightly on the high side, while Mr. Shane's is alleged to be $83.00 or slightly on the low side. In either case, we find these differences to not be material and see no basis for determining that a subgrouping should be applied to the Park tenants.
sections.question_synthetic: Do City Councilmembers Klove and Shane have conflicts of interest under the Political Reform Act in voting on a revised rent control ordinance for Westlake Village, given that they are both residents of Oak Forest Mobile Estates, the only mobilehome park subject to the ordinance?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC advises that Klove and Shane do not have disqualifying conflicts of interest because: (1) the direct effect test is not met since they have indicated no intention to sell and move; and (2) any speculative or remote financial effects do not constitute the type of reasonably foreseeable direct economic benefit that triggers conflict of interest provisions under the Political Reform Act.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: modern; LLM: Document is truncated at end of analysis section. Full conclusion section not visible in provided text. FPPC conducted extensive investigation including site visits and consultation with state housing agency. Key facts include two councilmembers are the only residents of the single mobilehome park subject to ordinance, creating apparent conflict, but analysis concludes effect is too indirect/speculative to disqualify.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: You seek written advice pursuant to Government Code Section 83114(b) as to whether or not Councilmembers Klove and Shane have any conflicts of interest relating to the proposed adoption of a revised rent control ordinance, for the City of Westlake Village.

CONCLUSION: (Fill in your "bottom line")

_____________________

22/ The "average" was calculated as the arithmetic mean by totalling the actual rents and dividing by 159 (number of rents listed).

23/ The "average" was calculated as the arithmetic mean by totalling the "market" rents in the appraisal and dividing by 162 (number of spaces).

24/ The "average" was calculated by subtracting actual average rents from market average rents.

25/ This "average" is the median for the "premiums," with 80 tenants having a lesser "premium" and 79 a greater "premium."
embedding.first_500_words: This memo contains detailed analysis of public generally issues, especially numerosity. Not discussed or accepted as policy because issue resolved on other bases. But research into numerosity is helpful. Patrick C. Coughlan Westlake Village City Attorney Richards, Watson, Dreyfuss & Gershon Thirty-Eighth Floor 333 South Hope Street Los Angeles, CA 90071 Re: Request for Written Advice, Our No. A-82-036 Dear Mr. Coughlan: Thank you for your request for written advice regarding Westlake Village City Councilmembers Bonnie Klove and Irwin Shane. In addition to our mutual correspondence and numerous telephone conversations, I have met with yourself and representatives of the other interested parties in this matter. They are: Mr. George Kimball, Attorney for Westpark Apartments Mr. Terry Dowdall, Attorney for Oak Forest Mobile Estates Mr. Raphael Chaikin, Co-Owner of Oak Forest Mobile Estates I have also received a telephonic communication from Mr. Henderson, a resident of Westlake Village. In addition, I personally visited Westlake Village and toured Oak Forest Mobile Estates, as well as visited Westpark Apartments. I have also received numerous written communications from Messrs. Kimball and Dowdall, as well as an appraisal of Oak Forest submitted to Mr. Chaikin by Real Estate Analysts of Newport, Inc. I have specifically invited counsel for all parties to submit arguments on the applicability of various provisions of the Political Reform Act and Commission interpretations thereof. All of the materials and data submitted have been reviewed by me and considered in the analysis of this matter. I have also consulted with, and received technical assistance as to legal interpretations of the California Mobilehome Residency Law (infra) from Ronald S. Javor, General Counsel, State Department of Housing and Community Development, the agency charged with the administration of that body of law. You seek written advice pursuant to Government Code Section 83114(b) as to whether or not Councilmembers Klove and Shane have any conflicts of interest relating to the proposed adoption of a revised rent control ordinance, for the City of Westlake Village. FACTS 1. Bonnie Klove and Irwin Shane are City Councilmembers in the City of Westlake Village, located in Los Angeles County. Westlake Village was incorporated as a result of a vote at the November 4, 1981, election. 2. The City is currently subject to a rent control ordinance originally adopted by Los Angeles County (prior to incorporation of Westlake Village) and subsequently re-adopted and extended by the Westlake Village City Council (Councilmembers Klove and Shane not participating). As extended, that ordinance will run through June 30, 1985. 3. Ms. Klove and Mr. Shane each reside in Oak Forest Mobile Estates (the "Park"), in Westlake Village. Oak Forest has a total of 162 spaces, 160 of which are currently occupied. Ms. Klove resides in Space 18, Mr. Shane in Space 160. 4. There is only one mobilehome park in Westlake Village - Oak Forest Mobile Estates. There is only one apartment complex in Westlake Village - Westpark Apartments. No other residential rental units exist which are subject to coverage of the existing rent
embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter addressing whether Councilmembers Bonnie Klove and Irwin Shane have conflicts of interest in voting on a revised rent control ordinance for Westlake Village, where they both reside as tenants in Oak Forest Mobile Estates. The FPPC concludes no disqualifying conflict exists because the financial effects are too remote or speculative and they have no stated intention to sell and relocate.
